Based on the retrieved evidence, hesperidin is primarily characterized as a bioflavonoid with known antioxidant and capillary-stabilizing properties, particularly in vascular health. While some indirect links exist through the broader context of flavonoids showing neuroprotective potential due to their ability to combat oxidative stress—an established factor in neurodegenerative diseases—the direct evidence regarding hesperidin specifically demonstrating neuroprotective effects in preclinical models or clinical settings is absent. Notably, the retrieved literature emphasizes other flavonoids like quercetin and diosmin more explicitly in relation to vascular stabilization and antioxidant actions, whereas no study details hesperidin's impact on neuronal survival, synaptic plasticity, or cognitive functions. Furthermore, although ziconotide is mentioned as having neuroprotective qualities, it represents a different class of agent unrelated to hesperidin. Therefore, without targeted mechanistic or clinical validation involving hesperidin alone, we cannot conclude it plays a confirmed role as a neuroprotective agent.
